title: Edward Said, My Grandfather, and the Problem of Home
description:
“Said's ‘Reflections on Exile’ summoned to mind the only 7 words my grandfather ever said to me about Ireland, where he had cousins he never knew: ‘I don’t think I’ll go back there.’”

The post Edward Said, My Grandfather, and the Problem of Home appeared first on Public Books.
hook: Said's exile haunts a family across generations—how a single refusal to return home unlocks everything about belonging.

title: Panahi’s Laboratory
description:
In Taxi (2015), Jafar Panahi stages a brief but haunting moment that, in retrospect, feels like the seed of his most recent film, It Was Just an Accident(2025).

The post Panahi’s Laboratory appeared first on The Point Magazine.
hook: Panahi's films stage accidents that feel inevitable—a cinema built from what happens when control vanishes.

title: Ṣẹ̀kẹ̀rẹ̀
description: Sometimes, love comes in a parcel of rice straws
hook: Love arrives wrapped in rice straw—a poem that lets one image do all the emotional work.

title: Saturday in San Antonio
description: I dropped my new friends off at the arena, a gray structure that blended into the surrounding parking lots. We waved goodbye, and they disappeared into the security line. Police on horseback wearing cowboy hats and actual spurs directed me away from the crowds, which added some Texas flair to the mundane act of sitting in traffic. hook: A Saturday in San Antonio where class, desire, and arena lights collide in one ordinary, piercing moment.

title: A Long-Lost Mother’s Embrace Is an Obliterative Fate
description:
“The Ominous Shaft” by Marlena Williams Cole set out early, when the fishermen of his hometown were just beginning to cast their nets into the sea. Claudia Bernard, the woman that Cole was journeying towards, had no idea that he was on his way. Cole made the drive from Astoria to Fortuna in one white-knuckled […]

The post A Long-Lost Mother’s Embrace Is an Obliterative Fate appea
hook: A man drives toward a mother he's never met, unknowing she waits—fate as erasure disguised as reunion.
